title: Lesson 5: Adjective Agreement: Gender, Number & Case   د صفت مطابقت: جنس، شمیر او حالت
description: Dive into one of Pashto's most morphologically complex systems: adjectival agreement. You will learn how adjectives inflect for gender, number, and grammatical case, while exploring colors and physical descriptions.

content.heading: Module 1: The Adjective Agreement System
lines: Welcome to Unit 5! You are about to tackle a major grammatical feature. In Pashto, adjectives inflect for the gender (masculine/feminine), number (singular/plural), and — critically — the grammatical case of the noun they modify[cite: 30].
Pashto has two major adjective classes. Class I adjectives change their endings: typically ending in [-ay] for masculine, [-a] for feminine, and [-i] for plural[cite: 30].
Class II adjectives are invariable or have restricted inflection. Examples include [munṭazir] (منتظر - waiting) and [māriz] (مریض - sick)[cite: 30].

content.text: Grammar Pro-Tip: The Oblique Case! This unit introduces the concept of the oblique case for the first time — a preview of the ergative system that will govern past-tense transitive sentences later[cite: 30]. When a noun is placed in a prepositional phrase (like 'pa ... ke'), its attached Class I adjective MUST change to match its oblique case form.

content.heading: Module 2: Colors and Attributes Vocabulary
lines: Let's expand your vocabulary with some essential Class I adjectives[cite: 30]:
Irregular Adjective Alert: The word [stər] (ستر) 'big' has a suppletive (irregular) plural form: [stern] (سترن)[cite: 30]!

content.heading: Sentence Structures: Direct vs. Oblique
lines: Let's observe how adjectives behave in different positions within our SOV framework[cite: 30].
Predicative: [kor spin da] (کور سپین دی) 'The house is white'[cite: 30].
Oblique agreement trigger: [pa spin kor ke] (په سپین کور کې) 'in the white house'. Here, both the noun and adjective are in the oblique case due to the preposition[cite: 30].

content.text: Cultural Context & Symbolism: Colour symbolism is deeply embedded in Pashtun culture. White ([spin]) symbolises purity and peace; black ([tor]) signifies mourning or enmity; and green ([shīn]) holds strong Islamic significance[cite: 30]. Sociolinguistic Note: Commenting on physical appearance is normal, but follows strict gender norms. Male speakers describing women requires care and sensitivity in dialogue design[cite: 30].

content.question: What happens to a Class I adjective when the noun it modifies is placed inside a prepositional phrase (e.g., using [pa ... ke])?
options: It moves to the end of the sentence to follow the verb.
It stays exactly the same, as adjectives do not change.
It must inflect to match the noun's oblique case.
It transforms into a Class II invariable adjective.
content.explanation: When a noun is governed by a preposition and enters the oblique case, the Class I adjective modifying it must also inflect to agree with that oblique case[cite: 30].

content.question: In Pashtun cultural symbolism, what does the color 'tor' (تور - black) typically represent?
content.explanation: In Pashtun culture, black ([tor]) is historically associated with mourning or enmity, whereas white ([spin]) represents purity and peace[cite: 30].
